tweets.text:
hi @nikitabier @elonmusk 

i really wish we could have a nice clean way for web apps to request authentication with a user’s X account when they’re opened in the X in-app browser on iOS/Android

that could allow cool things like X-native games that use X accounts for matchmaking, automatic live streaming to your X followers, and other awesome things

right now the only way to do it is to throw the user into the standard web OAuth flow, but when you do that the user must login from scratch, type user/password/2fa/etc, even though they’re already in the X app! it’s genuinely offensive

we should really have a way to deeplink into an enhanced native authentication flow when the webapp is loaded in the in-app webview

can you please implement this and let me be an early tester please 🥹👉👈

tweets.text:
the LLM shape-rotators are finally learning what the creatives and wordcels have known for almost a year: the chinese labs cannot be defeated

if all you do all day is obsessing over tweets from anthropic and openai updating their gay CLI tools, then you probably:

1. think you are uniquely smarter than everyone else
2. are completely oblivious to the fact that billions of people use instagram and tiktok (and douyin) every day and they actually have much more visibility than you do into AI progress because they’re consuming AI slop video 8 hours per day

chinese labs like bytelabs and kling have been absolutely DOMINATING video over the past 12 months. american labs are nowhere close. mind you pretty much every human being on the planet is consuming those AI videos all day every day

how many people are consuming content created by american labs? maybe 10% of that if i’m being extremely generous

the shape-rotators on X think the wordcels on ig/tiktok are “stupid” but they already knew for a while that chinese ai is absolutely killing it

tweets.text:
The Ethereum Foundation’s institutional privacy team just spun out into its own company.

This just went on my list of high-potential projects because the team behind it is unusually experienced for an early-stage privacy project.

They spent the past year working with banks, regulators, and asset managers through the Institutional Privacy Task Force, so they have undeniable experience in this sector.

Now they’re taking that work full-time under a for-profit company led by @oskarth.

Their research covers:

→ Private stablecoin transfers
→ Private bonds and settlement
→ Identity systems compatible with KYC

None of this is a finished privacy network yet. Their main goal is to turn those prototypes into systems institutions can use in production.

Btw, Bitmine + SharpLink + Joe Lubin backed the spinout.

A credible team with serious backers and a clear area of focus. I’m curious to see what they build for private finance on Ethereum.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Yesterday was my last day at the Ethereum Foundation.

Today we are launching EthSystems. We build confidential systems for institutional Ethereum.

I've spent close to a decade building privacy infrastructure in crypto: p2p messaging at @ethstatus, developing Waku protocols at
tweets.full_text:
The Ethereum Foundation’s institutional privacy team just spun out into its own company.

This just went on my list of high-potential projects because the team behind it is unusually experienced for an early-stage privacy project.

They spent the past year working with banks, regulators, and asset managers through the Institutional Privacy Task Force, so they have undeniable experience in this sector.

Now they’re taking that work full-time under a for-profit company led by @oskarth.

Their research covers:

→ Private stablecoin transfers
→ Private bonds and settlement
→ Identity systems compatible with KYC

None of this is a finished privacy network yet. Their main goal is to turn those prototypes into systems institutions can use in production.

Btw, Bitmine + SharpLink + Joe Lubin backed the spinout.

A credible team with serious backers and a clear area of focus. I’m curious to see what they build for private finance on Ethereum.

tweets.text:
New research from @dune and @1inch found that 85% of DeFi liquidity is underutilized at any given time.

That's up to $1.6B in deposits that are not earning effectively.

Reasons are pretty straightforward:

• Ranges are set too wide
• LP positions move out of range and stop earning fees
• No one can monitor and rebalance positions around the clock manually

The research tracked concentrated liquidity for 26 weeks across 4 DEXs and 7 chains.

On average, 29.5% of it was out of range at any given time. That translates to an estimated $185M to $195M in missed fees each year, based only on the capital deployed today.

It’s hard to take talk of "bringing trillions onchain" seriously while so much of DeFi’s existing capital remains underutilized.

The next stage of DeFi growth depends on making existing liquidity work more efficiently before bringing more of it onchain.

Then it can scale from there.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
1/ Across H1 2026, an average of 85% of concentrated-liquidity capital was underutilized and 29.5% sat outside the active price range. That works out to roughly $542M idle in a typical week.

New onchain research from Dune, produced for @1inch. https://t.co/VeI5SqEuWd

tweets.text: By checks, I mean people who check on other people to make sure they're performing well, and by balances, I mean balances of power. Even the most benevolent leaders are prone to becoming more autocratic, if for no other reason than because managing a lot of people and having limited time to do it requires them to make numerous difficult choices quickly, and they sometimes lose patience with arguments and issue commands instead. And most leaders are not so benevolent that they can be trusted to put the organization's interests ahead of their own. #principleoftheday

tweets.text: In the five‑step loop I teach, “diagnose” is the pivot point that turns a vague problem into a concrete lever for improvement. When you stop at step 2 and only name the symptom—say, “our portfolio underperformed this quarter”—you’re still not seeing the underlying cause. Sit with the discomfort long enough to ask: Is the issue a missing skill in our research team, a mis‑aligned incentive structure, or perhaps an external shock we didn’t anticipate? Use radical transparency and thoughtful disagreement to pull in other perspectives; remember that the best answer often lies outside your own head. Once you have a clear root cause, you can design a solution that hits the real source rather than just patching the surface. If the diagnosis reveals something you can’t fix yourself, escalate it or seek help—that’s part of the “best answer” principle. The key is to be specific about what you’re diagnosing, then iterate until the fix sticks. What kind of problems do you typically spot but hesitate to dig into deeply, and how might you bring in others to help you see the root cause?

Sit with the discomfort long enough to ask: Is the issue a missing skill in our research team, a mis‑aligned incentive structure, or perhaps an external shock we didn’t anticipate? Use radical transparency and thoughtful disagreement to pull in other perspectives; remember that the best answer often lies outside your own head. Once you have a clear root cause, you can design a solution that hits the real source rather than just patching the surface. If the diagnosis reveals something you can’t fix yourself, escalate it or seek help—that’s part of the “best answer” principle. The key is to be specific about what you’re diagnosing, then iterate until the fix sticks. What kind of problems do you typically spot but hesitate to dig into deeply, and how might you bring in others to help you see the root cause?

tweets.text:
One rarely gets a setup like tomorrow is presenting for $NFLX   You see it like a unicorn. Only briefly and in a fog. 

1) You need a totally washed out name in a multi-month collapse. 
2) You need sentiment to truly hit bottom.  No one has any positive arguments to make.
3) They print earnings on a Thursday so that the next day is an opex. 
4) You need massive volume ahead of the print to create massive, forced selling then buying the next day to offload option Delta.

All that is lining up. Not financial advice, but I am looking for a true capitulation flush tomorrow am, something that takes the price down around $65.  

Then I will buy something like $69/70 call debits for a few pennies, looking for them to 10x

Will follow up mañana with my buys. Likely to occur around 9:45 ET.

tweets.text:
wow it can fold tiny baby clothes? Where can I buy one asap?

Having capable robots in our homes will be incredibly liberating. We underestimate how much of life disappears into folding laundry, cleaning, tidying, and other repetitive chores (especially with young children!)

Our grandparents were freed from hours of domestic labor by washing machines and dryers. General-purpose home robots could be the next leap: giving millions of people back their most precious resource, time!
